mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers cell theory and classification (A), cell design and organization including macromolecules and morphological elements (B), functional aspects including membrane transport and cell movement (C), the cell cycle with mitosis and meiosis (D), and fertilization (E).
DNA as a macromolecular nucleic acid in cells (B.1.a), organized into chromosomes within the nuclear envelope (B.2.b), and replicated during genetic preparation for division (D.1).
Characterizes the cell as a molecular system including macromolecules (nucleic acids, proteins, polysaccharides) and small molecules (lipids, nucleotides, amino acids) in cells (B.1).
Proteins as macromolecules in cells (B.1.a), including actin filaments involved in cell movement and cytokinesis (C.4, D.2.b).
Eukaryotic cells with membrane-bound nucleus and organelles (A.2), distinguished from prokaryotes (B.2.c), with meiosis producing gametes (D.2.c).
Viruses compared to cells in the general classification, challenging the cell theory as obligate intracellular agents lacking autonomous metabolism (A.2.a).
Molecular structures of nucleic acids and proteins as the cell's molecular system (B.1) and genetic replication during cell growth (D.1).
Bacteria as prokaryotic cells (A.2.a) compared with eukaryotic cells in structure and organization (B.2.c).
Archaea as prokaryotic organisms distinguished from bacteria by membrane chemistry (A.2.a), relevant to procaryotic cell classification (B.2.c).
RNA as a nucleic acid macromolecule in cells (B.1.a) bridging genetic information storage and protein synthesis.
Polysaccharides as macromolecules in cells (B.1.a), with structural roles in cell walls (cellulose) and energy storage (glycogen).
Organisms represent cells in combination, with cohesion and communication forming tissues and multicellular organisms (C.5).
Cell theory as a foundational concept of life, with challenges and revisions in the light of later knowledge (A.1.b).
The origin of cells connects to the historical background of cell theory (A.1.a), with the emergence of cell membranes from prebiotic chemistry.
Meiosis as division of germ cells into gametes with half the genetic material (D.2.c) is the cellular basis of hereditary transmission.
Muscle cells as a classification of animal cells and tissues (A.2.b), with multinucleated myocytes as a specialized cell type.
Physiology at the cellular level covers functional aspects including cell matrix, membrane transport, nucleus-cytoplasm interplay, and cell movement (C).
Cell theory as one of biology's unifying themes, with the cell as the fundamental structural and functional unit of all life (A.1).
Genetics connects to the cell cycle through genetic replication (D.1) and meiosis producing gametes with genetic recombination (D.2.c).
